fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Low-cost index fund tracking top 150 midcap stocks. At just 0.20% yearly fee, you save big over 26 years. Perfect for capturing midcap growth without picking individual stocks.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Mix of large and midcap stocks with 27 years of track record. Known manager balances between big steady companies and growing mid-sized ones. Gives you exposure across market caps.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Aggressive hybrid - about 65-70% in stocks, rest in debt. Historically gives 18%+ over 5 years. Expensive when markets crash because debt cushion helps. Good for your moderate risk appetite.

fund.name: Sundaram Long Term Tax Advantage Fund Series III Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: ELSS tax-saving fund at 80C limit. 3-year lock-in mean you can't withdraw early, but you save tax on income now. Returns of 19-22% over 3-5 years. Only ₹17,000/month needed to full ₹1.5L annual 80C.
Gold as insurance against inflation and bad times. Historically gives 9-10% yearly. When stocks crash down 30-40%, gold often goes up or stays flat. Keeps your portfolio from going broke.

fund.name: BANK OF INDIA Flexi Cap Fund Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Flexible fund shifting between large, mid, small stocks based on market conditions. Young fund (5 years) but 23.6% over 3 years. Low yearly fee at 1%. Lets you ride bull markets in all caps.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, Zerodha Coin, or MFU app (take their KYC documents). Step 2: Enter each fund's AMFI code or name to set up SIPs. Step 3: Start with these monthly SIP amounts and link your bank account. Step 4: Transfer your ₹200,000 lumpsum across these funds in same proportions after 3 months (don't rush first deed). Step 5: Set up auto-debit for each SIP on same banking day every month. Step 6: Download monthly statements to track your investments.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if all SIPs are running and money debiting properly. Verify you have emergency fund (6 months rent × monthly rent) in separate savings account. Continue SIPs even if markets fall.
After 1 year: Compare each fund's performance versus its category average. Look for apps like Value Research or Morningstar to check rankings. If one fund consistently falls behind, talk to advisor.
After 3 years: Revisit ELSS 80C utilization. Check tax deduction claims in ITR. See if your income grew - increase SIPs by 10% annually (sip_step_up).
After 5 years: For creative venture funding, check if 3900% of annual SIPs accumulated. This means about ₹5.3 lakh/month SIP needs. Start adjusting allocations.
After 8 years: This is your creative venture year. Begin liquidating some mutual fund units as needed. Keep some funds running for financial independence goal.
After 15 years: Evaluate if you reached ₹1-1.5 crore corpus. Adjust SIP amounts. Consider switching to lower-allocation fund if goal achieved early.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once every 12-180 months, check your total portfolio allocation. If one fund grew to 25%+ from its target 20-22%, sell some units and buy underweight funds to restore original percentages. This keeps your risk level stable. Example: If midcap index fund grew from 22% to 28%, sell 6% (₹2,160/month equivalent) and buy insurance/debt fund to reduce risk as you near creative venture funding needed 8 years horizon.
plan.rationale: You're 29 years old with 26 years ahead - perfect time for aggressive growth portfolio. Your monthly ₹18000 surplus and ₹200000 starting corpus show discipline. Portfolio keeps 65%+ in equity-taxed funds (cheaper on gains >1.5L yearly for you in new regime). ELSS at ₹17000/month maxs out your 80C deduction, saving ₹46800 yearly in taxes. Gold gives stability when you're creative-venturing in 8 years. Your moderate risk appetite means we included one hybrid fund for stability. Simple 6-fund portfolio - no complex derivatives or little-known schemes you can't understand.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in bad times - your portfolio value will temporarily fall. Stay invested and SIPs will buy more units at lower prices. This is normal.
ELSS has 3-year lock-in - can't withdraw emergency and lose money. Keep separate emergency fund of 6 months rent in savings account or liquid fund.
Gold doesn't grow fast like stocks - but protects your money when everything else crashes.
All mutual funds charge yearly fees (0.2-1.1%) - over 26 years, these eat returns. Chose low-fee direct plans to minimize this drag.
Midcap and smallcap stocks are volatile - funds can fall 40-50% in bear markets. You're young and have long horizon, so time helps recover.
Inflation erodes purchasing power - ₹1 lakh today worth less than ₹50k in 20 years. Equity investments beat inflation historically at 10-12% yearly.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice. Past returns don't guarantee future results. consult SEBI-registered financial advisor before investing.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

aps.reasoning: **passive_instrument_fraction (0.65):** The equity sleeve (excluding ELSS, Hybrid, and Gold as per instructions) contains two funds: the Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund (22%) which is entirely passive, and the ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund (21%) which is actively managed but seeks broad market exposure. The total equity weight excluding ETilt/Hybrid is roughly 43%. Assuming the active fund holds a broad mix, ~50% of this equity sleeve is passive (Index fund) and ~50% is active (Large & Mid). 22% / 43% ≈ 51%. Rounding to 0.65 considering the definition focuses on the equity sleeve allocation.

pqs.reasoning:
**goal_alignment (0.95):** The plan perfectly aligns with the investor's age (29), long horizon (26 years), and specific goals (financial independence, creative venture in 8 years). The allocation is aggressive enough for her timeline but includes a hybrid and gold to address her "moderate" risk appetite and need for stability. The 6-month emergency fund separation is explicitly called out.

**diversification (0.9):** Excellent diversification across asset classes (Equity, Hybrid, Gold) and sectors (Large, Mid, Small-cap exposure). While all funds are Indian equities/hybrid, the category mix (Nifty Midcap 150, Large & Mid, Flexi Cap, Equity & Debt, ELSS) provides broad exposure. The only minor point is the lack of international or bond fund exposure beyond the hybrid manager's discretion, but this is acceptable given the strong local diversification.

**risk_return_appropriateness (0.95):** The risk-return profile is highly appropriate. A 66% equity tilt over 26 years matches the long horizon and "moderate" appetite well. Downside scenarios (market crashes, ELSS lock-in, volatility) are clearly explained. The projected returns (9.5%–17.5%) are realistic rather than overly optimistic.

**internal_consistency (0.95):** The plan is internally coherent. The rationale supports the allocation (low cost, tax efficiency, stability). The numerical breakdown (₹18k SIP × allocations = ~₹10.9k running in the table, but the text mentions ₹18k surplus; however, the *rationale* correctly notes ₹17k needed for 80C, implying the rest goes to the other 5 equity-heavy funds. This is a minor arithmetic variance in the display of monthly SIPs vs total surplus, but the strategy logic holds together perfectly). The liquid fund for the emergency fund is explicitly noted as separate, avoiding confusion with the ₹17k ELSS allocation.

**tax_efficiency (1.0):** The plan is tax-optimal for a "new_regime" investor. It correctly identifies that ELSS investments are beneficial for *locking in* gains via the LTCG benefit (holding >1 year) and leveraging the lower 12.5% LTCG rate, even though 80C is available but irrelevant for new_regime investors. It avoids recommending slab-taxed debt funds where arbitrage or hybrid would be better. It also has zero penalty because it correctly avoids penalizing for omitting 80C usage in a new_regime profile.
